 Details
 -------
 
-The following type aliases are defined in both C and C++, if supported
-by the compiler:
-- ``GFloat16`` --> e.g. ``_Float16``
+The type ``_Float16`` is defined in both C and C++, if supported by
+the compiler. This is the type name that C will (most likely) use in
+the future. Modern GCC releases provide this type already. OTher
+compilers might support this type under a different name, and in this
+case, GDAL will define ``_Float16`` as type alias.","path":"doc/source/development/rfc/rfc100_float16_support.rst","commit_id":"1eb51418717c633ed8384dd16d7807a393948ade","original_commit_id":"1eb51418717c633ed8384dd16d7807a393948ade","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"At this point it's fairly certain that the type will be named `_Float16`. The `setdevenv.sh` script contains code to check whether it is called or sourced by the shell:
```
called=$_

if [[ $BASH_VERSION && $(realpath $called) == $(realpath "$0") ]]; then
    echo "Script should be sourced with '. $0', instead of run."
    exit 1
fi
```

This test fails for me for two reasons:
- The shell is called `-bash`, with a leading hyphen, and `realpath` aborts because it does not know about a `-b` option
- Fixing this by adding `--` before the argument the test still fails, probably because of the leading hyphen


### Steps to reproduce the issue

I source the script via
```
cd build
(. ../scripts/setdevenv.sh)
```

### Versions and provenance

Current master branch as of today

### Additional context

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920","id":2370982081,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6NUlDB","number":54920,"title":"maximum(abs, ::Complex{Int}; dims=...) fails with an internal error","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-24T19:53:29Z","updated_at":"2024-06-24T19:53:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This code fails:
```
julia> A = Complex{Int}.(rand(Complex{Int8}, 2, 2, 2));
julia> maximum(abs, A; dims=(1, 2))
```

The reason is that Julia wants to store the result of `abs(::Complex{T})` as type `T`. This fails for integer `T` since `abs` returns a real number (not an integer) when applied to a complex number.

The error is probably in the line
```
_realtype(::Union{typeof(abs),typeof(abs2)}, T) = _realtype(T)
```
in `reducedim.jl`. This lines doesn't take into account that `abs` may return a type that's different from its input.

The error is
```
ERROR: InexactError: Int64(137.0109484676316)
Stacktrace:
  [1] Int64
    @ ./float.jl:912 [inlined]
  [2] convert
    @ ./number.jl:7 [inlined]
  [3] setindex!
    @ ./array.jl:1024 [inlined]
  [4] setindex!
    @ ./multidimensional.jl:698 [inlined]
  [5] _mapreducedim!(f::typeof(abs), op::typeof(max), R::Array{Int64, 3}, A::Array{Complex{Int64}, 3})
    @ Base ./reducedim.jl:311
  [6] mapreducedim!(f::Function, op::Function, R::Array{Int64, 3}, A::Array{Complex{Int64}, 3})
    @ Base ./reducedim.jl:324
  [7] _mapreduce_dim(f::Function, op::Function, ::Base._InitialValue, A::Array{Complex{Int64}, 3}, dims::Tuple{Int64, Int64})
    @ Base ./reducedim.jl:371
  [8] mapreduce
    @ ./reducedim.jl:357 [inlined]
  [9] _maximum
    @ ./reducedim.jl:1039 [inlined]
 [10] #maximum#837
    @ ./reducedim.jl:1011 [inlined]
 [11] top-level scope
    @ REPL[64]:1
```

As a work-around one can write `maximum(abs ∘ identity, ...)`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54920/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

content"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-07-11T22:23:54Z","updated_at":"2024-05-29T13:49:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I found the gdalmdiminfo/translate APIs recently and tried to figure out through the docs which drivers support the multidimensional API, but couldn't find these information easily. While there's a list for raster and vector drivers, it would also be nice to add a list of drivers that support the multidimensional API or something similar. From searching for OpenMultiDim in the code, it seems they are:
- GRIB
- HDF4/5
- netCDF
- ZARR

Is this correct/complete?

Not sure what the best approach would be? Adding a column to the raster driver page? Adding a completely new page similar to the list of raster drivers?

If this is decided to be usedul, I'm happy to create a PR for it. But want to check back first whether this is welcome and what the preferred approach would be. The Zarr file format automatically converts 64-bit integer attributes into 64-bit floating-point attributes. I assume the reason is that attributes are stored as JSON and JSON doesn't guarantee that 64-bit integers can safely round-trip. However, reading 64-bit integer attributes is supported, probably in case another packages creates such an attribute.

I would like to have a way (e.g. an option when creating a file, or when creating a multidim array?) to write 64-bit integer attributes as integers in JSON, without converting them to a floating-point number.

### Additional context

If you agree this is a good idea I might be able to contribute such a change.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/OSGeo/gdal/issues/10062/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/OSGeo/gdal/issues/10062/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

What does this PR do?

The Zarr file format stores attributes in JSON. This PR stores int64 values as integer JSON values instead of converting them to floating-point values.

## What are related issues/pull requests?

https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/issues/10062

 - [ ] Add documentation
 - [ ] Updated Python API documentation (swig/include/python/docs/)
 - [ ] Review

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-03-26T15:15:38Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T00:14:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"From calculus we know `(1 + 1/n) ^ n` converges to Euler's `e`.

```
julia> n = 2^52
4503599627370496

julia> (1 + 1 / n) ^ n
2.7182818284590446
```
that's quite good! But
```
julia> @fastmath (1 + 1 / n) ^ n
ERROR: InexactError: trunc(Int32, 4503599627370496)
Stacktrace:
 [1] throw_inexacterror(::Symbol, ::Vararg{Any})
   @ Core ./boot.jl:772
 [2] checked_trunc_sint
   @ ./boot.jl:786 [inlined]
 [3] toInt32
   @ ./boot.jl:823 [inlined]
 [4] Int32
   @ ./boot.jl:913 [inlined]
 [5] convert
   @ ./number.jl:7 [inlined]
 [6] cconvert
   @ ./essentials.jl:662 [inlined]
 [7] pow_fast(x::Float64, y::Int64)
   @ Base.FastMath ./fastmath.jl:286
 [8] top-level scope
   @ REPL[31]:1
```
Reason is that `Base.FastMath.pow_fast` calls the llvm intrinsic `llvm.powi.f64.i32`, for integer second argument and tries to convert the integer to an `Int32`, which fails.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53857/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53857/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2035838893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53857#issuecomment-2035838893","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53857","id":2035838893,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM55WG-t","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-04T00:14:53Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T00:14:53Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I looked a bit into this. I think the main difference is that `Base.power_by_squaring` is so large that it doesn't get inlined, and the benchmark code then involves an additional function call. When I add `@inline` to `Base.power_by_squaring` then the performance difference becomes smaller.

With `@inline`, the generated assembler code is functionally identical, but a performance difference remains. I do not understand this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2035838893/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

and related functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":48,"created_at":"2016-02-05T19:52:14Z","updated_at":"2024-02-20T14:55:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I ran into this very strange bug while exploring the new multithreading stuff. The smallest reproducible case I can come up with:

``` julia
using Base.Threads

function right(v)
    ws = ntuple(w->Vector{Int}(length(v)), nthreads())
    @threads for t in 1:nthreads()
        w = ws[t]
        for i in 1:length(v)
            w[i] = v[i]
        end
    end
    w2 = 1 # unused binding to an unused variable name
    return ws
end

function wrong(v)
    ws = ntuple(w->Vector{Int}(length(v)), nthreads())
    @threads for t in 1:nthreads()
        w = ws[t]
        for i in 1:length(v)
            w[i] = v[i]
        end
    end
    w = 1 # unused binding to the name of the previously used loop variable
    return ws
end
```

Note that the _only_ difference between the above two functions is `w2 = 1` and `w = 1`, and that neither variable is actually used after being assigned.

Running the above in the REPL:

``` julia
julia> v = collect(1:3)

julia> right(v) # gives the correct output every time
([1,2,3],[1,2,3],[1,2,3],[1,2,3])

julia> wrong(v)
([4624170704,4658607408,4624170720],[4563189968,4574717712,4563189968],[1,2,3],[1,2,3])

julia> wrong(v)
([1,2,3],[1,2,3],[1,2,3],[4624170800,4658607472,4624170816])

julia> wrong(v)
([1,2,3],[1,2,3],[1,2,3],[1,2,3])

julia> wrong(v)
([1,4658458992,4624197552],[1,2,3],[4624197648,4624197664,4624197680],[1,2,3])
```

It appears that some iterations of the multithreaded loop in `wrong(v)` aren't always being executed, or possibly that the binding of the loop variable `w` to `ws[t]` is broken. Either way, one can induce the correct behavior by making sure the post-loop binding to `1` doesn't match the loop variable name. 

I thought maybe that this was a escaping/scoping bug in [`_threadsfor`](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/641f923893d2a64eb6e241e4e7c11c835c407b1a/base/threadingconstructs.jl#L10), but I didn't see anything that jumped out at me.

``` julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 0.5.0-dev+2502
Commit baf336f (2016-02-04 18:01 UTC)
Platform Info:
  System: Darwin (x86_64-apple-darwin13.4.0)
  CPU: Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-4288U CPU @ 2.60GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  BLAS: libopenblas (USE64BITINT DYNAMIC_ARCH NO_AFFINITY Haswell)
  LAPACK: libopenblas64_
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-3.7.1

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808","id":2152410897,"node_id":"I_kwDOBOhtSc6ASy8R","number":3808,"title":"Make spinners spin more slowly","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-24T17:19:40Z","updated_at":"2024-02-24T17:19:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was updating Julia packages over an unstable internet connection and I didn't like how fast the spinners were running. I think they spin about twice per second.

I looked at the Pkg.jl code, and I found that setting the environment variable `CI=true` would disable all progress output. That's good, but I was looking for something fancier – just slowing down the progress output, not eliminating it.

I think it would be possible to make the spinner speed configurable with a rather small code change. I think this line https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/e7e8ce38359330441b1340046add367761035f69/src/API.jl#L1389 defines how fast the spinners are.

One could add a new global variable
```Julia
const PRECOMPILE_SPINNER_INTERVAL = Ref(0.1)
```
to the code, and then create the Timer via `Timer(0; interval=PRECOMPILE_SPINNER_INTERVAL[])`. This would allow changing the spinners by setting a global variable. A similar mechanism is already used in `MiniProgressBars.jl` where two variables are defined https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/e7e8ce38359330441b1340046add367761035f69/src/MiniProgressBars.jl#L21-L22

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3808","id":2152410897,"node_id":"I_kwDOBOhtSc6ASy8R","number":3808,"title":"Make spinners spin more slowly","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-02-24T17:19:40Z","updated_at":"2024-02-24T18:26:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was updating Julia packages over an unstable internet connection and I didn't like how fast the spinners were running. I think they spin about twice per second.

I looked at the Pkg.jl code, and I found that setting the environment variable `CI=true` would disable all progress output. That's good, but I was looking for something fancier – just slowing down the progress output, not eliminating it.

I think it would be possible to make the spinner speed configurable with a rather small code change. I think this line https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/e7e8ce38359330441b1340046add367761035f69/src/API.jl#L1389 defines how fast the spinners are.

One could add a new global variable
```Julia
const PRECOMPILE_SPINNER_INTERVAL = Ref(0.1)
```
to the code, and then create the Timer via `Timer(0; interval=PRECOMPILE_SPINNER_INTERVAL[])`. This would allow changing the spinners by setting a global variable. A similar mechanism is already used in `MiniProgressBars.jl` where two variables are defined https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/e7e8ce38359330441b1340046add367761035f69/src/MiniProgressBars.jl#L21-L22

Julia"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-01-22T17:15:02Z","updated_at":"2024-01-27T20:53:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello. I noticed today that if you have a matrix of `Diagonal` type, then the eigenvector matrix (from `eigvecs` or `eigen`) is not of type `Diagonal`. Wouldn't it make more sense for the eigenvectors to match the type of the input matrix, when possible?

For example, if I have `A = Diagonal(rand(10))`, then `eigvecs(A;kwarg...)` should return a `Diagonal` (e.g. `one(A)`), but `eigvecs(Matrix(A);kwarg...)` should return a `Matrix` (e.g. `one(Matrix(A))`), and so on. The same changes would be needed for `eigen(...)`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53007/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53007/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1913332658","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53007#issuecomment-1913332658","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53007","id":1913332658,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5yCyOy","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-27T20:53:35Z","updated_at":"2024-01-27T20:53:35Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Isn't eigenvector calculation type unstable anyway? `eigvecs` checks whether the matrix is symmetric (by checking the values in the matrix), and if so, returns a real-valued result. Thus a dependence on a keyword argument shouldn't matter.
```Julia
julia> A = randn(4,4);

julia> typeof(eigvecs(A))
Matrix{ComplexF64} (alias for Array{Complex{Float64}, 2})

julia> typeof(eigvecs(A+A'))
Matrix{Float64} (alias for Array{Float64, 2})
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3686","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3686/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3686/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3686/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3686","id":1977406006,"node_id":"I_kwDOBOhtSc513NI2","number":3686,"title":"Allow comments in `[compat]` entries","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-11-04T15:51:34Z","updated_at":"2023-11-04T15:51:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Ensuring that the `[compat]` version bounds for a package are correct can be tedious, and often requires experience and historic knowledge. It is thus important to be able to document why a particular compat entry is there.

I am thinking of comments along these lines: "version x.y.z doesn't work, try again after the next minor release", or "this breaks on BSD", or "doesn't handle snarfblurbs larger than 2 GByte", etc. This allows maintainers to codify their knowledge about version bounds, what was tried in the past, what the symptoms were, whether it is worthwhile to relax a version bound, etc.

It would be convenient to have these comments in the `[compat]` section, attaches to a particular version bound. Just spinning yarn – maybe such a syntax might work?
```toml
Dependency = "<1.2.3   # function `flexdup` was accidentally removed in 1.2.3"
Misery = "=4.5.5   # keep this fixed, Misery doesn't use semver"
Chirp1 = "0.1, 0.2, 0.3   # 0.4 doesn't work, try 0.5 next"
Chirp2 = "0.1, 0.2, 0.3   # try 0.4 once it's available"
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52067","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52067/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52067/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52067/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52067","id":1981797390,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM52H9QO","number":52067,"title":"`Undefined symbols: ___truncsfbf2` when building on macOS","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-11-07T16:50:58Z","updated_at":"2023-11-07T16:50:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am building the current master version of Julia from scratch on macOS (`Darwin redshift.pi.local 23.1.0 Darwin Kernel Version 23.1.0: Mon Oct  9 21:27:27 PDT 2023; root:xnu-10002.41.9~6/RELEASE_X86_64 x86_64 i386 Darwin`). I see this error:
```
$ make
    LINK usr/lib/libjulia-internal.1.11.0.dylib
ld: Undefined symbols:
  ___truncsfbf2, referenced from:
      _julia__truncsfbf2 in runtime_intrinsics.o
      _julia__truncdfbf2 in runtime_intrinsics.o
clang++: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
make[1]: *** [Makefile:388: /Users/eschnett/src/julia-master/usr/lib/libjulia-internal.1.11.0.dylib] Error 1
make: *** [Makefile:97: julia-src-release] Error 2
```

I have both GCC and Clang installed via MacPorts.

It seems that Julia uses this Clang (`which clang++`: `/opt/local/bin/clang++`). I checked, and it seems that only GCC provides this function (`/opt/local/lib/gcc13/gcc/x86_64-apple-darwin23/13.2.0/libgcc.a:truncsfbf2.o: 0000000000000000 T ___truncsfbf2`), but Clang does not provide it.

```
$ clang++ --version
clang version 17.0.4
Target: x86_64-apple-darwin23.1.0
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /opt/local/libexec/llvm-17/bin
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51419","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51419/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51419/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51419/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51419","id":1907584679,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5xs26n","number":51419,"title":"Sparse matrices: Catastrophic fill-in when solving transpose","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-09-21T18:56:24Z","updated_at":"2023-09-21T18:56:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am solving a linear system where the LHS is a diagonal matrix and the RHS is the transpose of a sparse matrix. The resulting sparse matrix has all its zeros filled in. I show an example where I first create a diagonal LHS and a sparse RHS, and the solve with both the sparse matrix and its transpose. Note the `with 9 stored entries` in the output; this should not be.

Brief testing indicates that this is also related to rational numbers of big integers.

```
julia> diag = Diagonal([big(1)//1,1,1])
3×3 Diagonal{Rational{BigInt}, Vector{Rational{BigInt}}}:
 1  ⋅  ⋅
 ⋅  1  ⋅
 ⋅  ⋅  1

julia> sp = sparse(Diagonal([1,1,1]))
3×3 SparseMatrixCSC{Int64, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
 1  ⋅  ⋅
 ⋅  1  ⋅
 ⋅  ⋅  1

julia> diag \ sp
3×3 SparseMatrixCSC{Rational{BigInt}, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
 1  ⋅  ⋅
 ⋅  1  ⋅
 ⋅  ⋅  1

julia> diag \ sp'
3×3 SparseMatrixCSC{Rational{BigInt}, Int64} with 9 stored entries:
 1  0  0
 0  1  0
 0  0  1
```

julia> versioninfo()

OpenSSL","user":{"login":"fxcoudert","id":1980544,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5ODA1NDQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1980544?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert","html_url":"https://github.com/fxcoudert","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fxcoudert/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":550946078,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw1NTA5NDYwNzg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/external%20dependencies","name":"external dependencies","color":"A87582","default":false,"description":"Involves LLVM, OpenBLAS, or other linked libraries"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":25,"created_at":"2023-02-26T12:58:50Z","updated_at":"2023-09-27T16:29:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"_There has already been an issue that proposed migration to BoringSSL (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45856), which is not what I propose here._

- Julia already depends on OpenSSL
- Julia currently depends on mbedTLS for:
  - libssh2, on all targets
  - libgit2, on Linux/FreeBSD
  - curl, on Linux/FreeBSD
- All three dependencies could migrate to OpenSSL
- We would cut down the dependency tree
- Avoid relying on an old branch of mbedTLS

From a security perspective, this seems great (fewer updates to manage, and track record shows mbedTLS is frequently behind in Julia).

**Are there any downsides to migrating to OpenSSL and removing mbedTLS?**

If not, and the idea has support, I volunteer to handle the migration PRs in Yggdrasil and julia.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48799/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48799/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1737723692","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48799#issuecomment-1737723692","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48799","id":1737723692,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5nk48s","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-27T16:29:08Z","updated_at":"2023-09-27T16:29:08Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The packages currently using mbedTLS are:
- IOAPI
- NetCDFF
- TempestRemap
- samtools
- Aria2
- AzStorage
- VMEC
- LibCURL
- LibSSH2
- LibGit2
- libjulia

Of these, I assume that only the last four are important for Julia itself. Many of the others are only using mbedTLS to stay transitively consistent.

The web page https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/ documents many Julia versions, including version v1.9.2 and v1.11-dev. However, there is no documentation for v1.10-beta available.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Go to 'https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/'
2. Click on the "version" drop-down menu
3. Scroll to the bottom

**Expected behavior**
Since documentation for the development version is available I expect documentation for the current beta version – which is easily available for download – do be available as well.

   2: 0x7fffed64650e - main'''
```

I am running on Frontier, and HPC system at OLCF <https://www.olcf.ornl.gov/frontier/>. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316","id":1661275211,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5jBQxL","number":49316,"title":"Synchronizing GC calls in distributed computing","user":{"login":"simonbyrne","id":1692009,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTIwMDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1692009?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne","html_url":"https://github.com/simonbyrne","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/simonbyrne/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":9159719,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTU5NzE5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parallel","name":"parallel","color":"f7e102","default":false,"description":"Parallel or distributed computation"},{"id":195227131,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxOTUyMjcxMzE=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/GC","name":"GC","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Garbage collector"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-04-10T19:56:00Z","updated_at":"2023-04-18T15:43:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We're running large distributed jobs with tightly synchronized communication (halo exchanges for PDE solvers), and GC pauses cause catastrophic failures in scaling: 1 process calls the GC, which causes all neighboring processes to wait, which their neighbors and so on. A 600ms GC pause in what is a 10ms synchronization cycle quickly adds a lot of overhead. This gets worse at higher process counts, as the probablility of any 1 process invoking the GC will increase. We're using MPI.jl, but the same would apply with Distributed.jl.

At the moment our only real solution is to (a) minimize the number of allocations which occur, and (b) manually trigger the GC on all processes at the same time, which we do this by simply calling the GC at fixed intervals. This means that lots of manual tuning is required to figure out an appropriate GC interval.

Would it be possible to have some mechanism to query the memory pressure heuristics? In that way, if we know that the GC is likely to be invoked soon on a single process, we could then manually trigger it on all processes at the same time.

I'm also open to other suggestions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316/reactions","total_count":11,"+1":11,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1513388583","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316#issuecomment-1513388583","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49316","id":1513388583,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5aNHon","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-18T15:43:44Z","updated_at":"2023-04-18T15:43:44Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This sounds reasonable. I'd go one step further and suggest a GC hook that would allow triggering GCs on all nodes in a reliable manner, not just as a heuristic.

In a true distributed systems (probably not via MPI.jl, but with higher level interfaces) there will also be references to remote objects. A local GC can never determine whether these can be freed: Such objects are only freed when the finalizers on the remote references run, triggering a message that will reduce the object's reference count. It might also take several local GC cycles to actually reclaim such objects. In the long term it would be nice if the GC heuristics would take this scenario into account as well, probably via global statistics such as "sum of all heap bytes allocated on all nodes since the last global GC".","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1513388583/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/430","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/430/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/430/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/430/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/430","id":1437535471,"node_id":"I_kwDOEE6KR85Vrwzv","number":430,"title":"juliaup does not support PowerPC","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-11-06T20:50:57Z","updated_at":"2022-11-06T20:50:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I tried to use juliaup on the OLCF HPC system "Summit". This fails:

```sh
$ curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh

info: downloading installer
curl: (22) The requested URL returned error: 404
juliaup: command failed: downloader https://julialang-s3.julialang.org/juliaup/bin/juliainstaller-1.7.24-powerpc64le-unknown-linux-gnu /tmp/tmp.QgYYKkrw9P/juliainstaller powerpc64le-unknown-linux-gnu
```

It seems that the url `https://julialang-s3.julialang.org/juliaup/bin/juliainstaller-1.7.24-powerpc64le-unknown-linux-gnu` does not exist, presumably because this CPU architecture is not supported.

t.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-09-06T17:44:26Z","updated_at":"2022-09-06T17:47:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This fails:
```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> using SparseArrays
julia> kron(Diagonal(sparsevec([1])), Bidiagonal([2], Int[], :U))
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching deleteat!(::SparseVector{Int64, Int64}, ::Int64)
```

The problem is that this `kron` method calls `deleteat!`, which does not exist for sparse vectors.

Since sparse vectors are immutable, `deleteat!` cannot be implemented. (A `deleteat` could be implemented, though.)

I think the solution is to call `deleteat!` only when the respective vector type holding the diagonal elements is mutable, or (to be safe) when it is `Vector`.

This error was introduced by https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46463.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46654/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46654/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1238470908","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46654#issuecomment-1238470908","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46654","id":1238470908,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5J0ZD8","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-06T17:47:13Z","updated_at":"2022-09-06T17:47:13Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"`Tridiagonal` matrices have the same issue. `SymTridiagonal` matrices are fine.

Another (probably long-term) way to address this would be to allow an extra stored element in `Bidiagonal` and `Tridiagonal` matrices. This is already allowed in `SymTridiagonal` matrices.

```

Julia"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2021-08-30T19:54:17Z","updated_at":"2022-09-08T16:57:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was recently porting a Matlab example to julia and I realized there's no direct equivalence.

I extended the floor function in my code snippet by doing:
```julia
import Base.floor
floor(x::Complex) = floor(real(x)::Real) + 1im*floor(imag(x)::Real)
```

I'm not sure if this would be a welcome addition, but it would surely make Julia code more similar to other well-used scientific software.

In this case, `magnitude(x::Complex) = max(abs(real(x)), abs(imag(x)))`, and the fractionality constraint holds. The infinity-norm is arguably more suited for Gaussian integers, which are compatible with the result type `Complex{Int}`. (What one loses this way is the radius/phase representation of complex numbers.)

This definition extends nicely to other vector spaces. If one treats complex numbers as a vector space over reals, this is a natural extension.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1240978043/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

numbers"},{"id":208968668,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMDg5Njg2Njg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/good%20first%20issue","name":"good first issue","color":"0052cc","default":true,"description":"Indicates a good issue for first-time contributors to Julia"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":18,"created_at":"2020-09-03T13:51:16Z","updated_at":"2022-09-13T16:43:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As [discussed on discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/extend-mod-first-argument-to-gaussian-integers), this seems like the only sensible definition:
```
mod(z::Complex{<:Integer}, n::Integer) = Complex(mod(real(z), n), mod(imag(z), n))
```
and similarly for `div`, `rem` (and probably `divrem`).   

Should be easy to create a patch (much simpler than #35374): just add the 1-line definitions analogous to the one above, docs, news, and a test.

(In addition, there is complex multiplication.)

Scalar multiplication and scalar division are inherited from being a vector space. div/mod/rem can be inherited in the same way. The special structure imbued by complex multiplication does not play a role here.

Other languages (e.g. Mathematica) make this choice as well. It's also a natural choice for Julia, given that complex numbers are represented as a vector of its real and imaginary components.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1245674045/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Julia define the generic (two-argument) `log` as `log(x, y) = log(y) / log(x)`. This could be improved (to be slightly faster and slightly more accurate) by changing this to `log(x, y) = log2(y) / log2(x)`.

For example:
```Julia
julia> log(1000000) / log(100)
2.9999999999999996

julia> log2(1000000) / log2(100)
3.0
```

Julia define the generic (two-argument) `log` as `log(x, y) = log(y) / log(x)`. This could be improved (to be slightly faster and slightly more accurate) by changing this to `log(x, y) = log2(y) / log2(x)`.

```Julia
B = Diagonal(sparsevec([1],[1//1]))
W = Diagonal([1//1])

```

The expression `B * inv(W)`, which is mathematically identical, does not fail.

```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> using StaticArrays
julia> map(SMatrix{1,1}, Diagonal([1, 2]))
2×2 Matrix{SMatrix{1, 1, Int64, 1}}:
 [1;;]  [0;;]
 [0;;]  [2;;]
```
The result of `map` is a dense matrix (`Matrix`) instead of a diagonal matrix (`Diagonal`).

Although `iszero(SMatrix{1,1}(1))`, `map` converts the matrix to a dense matrix.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292","id":1332671674,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5PbvS6","number":46292,"title":"`map` for `Diagonal` matrix is inefficient","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-08-09T03:54:19Z","updated_at":"2022-08-09T15:47:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Applying a function to a `Diagonal` matrix can convert it to a dense matrix without need:

```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> using StaticArrays
julia> map(SMatrix{1,1}, Diagonal([1, 2]))
2×2 Matrix{SMatrix{1, 1, Int64, 1}}:
 [1;;]  [0;;]
 [0;;]  [2;;]
```
The result of `map` is a dense matrix (`Matrix`) instead of a diagonal matrix (`Diagonal`).

Although `iszero(SMatrix{1,1}(1))`, `map` converts the matrix to a dense matrix.

See e.g. https://github.com/JuliaSparse/SparseArrays.jl/blob/090474bec9de69d2573e816a6187568822f1d85a/src/higherorderfns.jl#L160 for sparse arrays:
```Julia
function _noshapecheck_map!(f::Tf, C::SparseVecOrMat, A::SparseVecOrMat, Bs::Vararg{SparseVecOrMat,N}) where {Tf,N}
    fofzeros = f(_zeros_eltypes(A, Bs...)...)
    fpreszeros = _iszero(fofzeros)
    return fpreszeros ? _map_zeropres!(f, C, A, Bs...) :
                        _map_notzeropres!(f, fofzeros, C, A, Bs...)
end
```
Diagonal arrays and their friends should do the same.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1209560246/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

One can define types where the result of `sin(zero(T))` has the same problem:
```Julia
struct IntFillMatrix
    val::Int
end
```
and its values are equivalent to
```Julia
Base.convert(::Type{Matrix}, ifm::IntFillMatrix) = ifm.val * [1 1; 1 1]
```
Applying `sin` to it, we need to convert to floating point. Our type doesn't support that, so we implement
```Julia
Base.sin(ifm::IntFillMatrix) = sin(Matrix(ifm))
```

I agree that my example is contrived, and I haven't encountered this problem in practice. However, given that sparse arrays also currently don't handle this, I would treat this as a separate problem, and implement the sparse array optimization to diagonal (and related) matrices as well","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1210066733/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46292","id":1332671674,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5PbvS6","number":46292,"title":"`map` for `Diagonal` matrix is inefficient","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2022-08-09T03:54:19Z","updated_at":"2022-08-10T21:54:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Applying a function to a `Diagonal` matrix can convert it to a dense matrix without need:

```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> using StaticArrays
julia> map(SMatrix{1,1}, Diagonal([1, 2]))
2×2 Matrix{SMatrix{1, 1, Int64, 1}}:
 [1;;]  [0;;]
 [0;;]  [2;;]
```
The result of `map` is a dense matrix (`Matrix`) instead of a diagonal matrix (`Diagonal`).

Although `iszero(SMatrix{1,1}(1))`, `map` converts the matrix to a dense matrix.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315","id":1336025233,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5PoiCR","number":46315,"title":"Support complex rational infinity","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-11T14:15:05Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T14:15:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Rational numbers (`Rational{Int}`) support infinity in the sense that `inv(0//1)` is handled well. This is not the case for complex rational numbers, i.e. `inv(Complex(0//1))` is not handled; instead, it reports an `ERROR: DivideError: integer division error`.

I understand the reason for this. In the standard conformal completion of the complex number plan, there is just a single point at infinity. This is different from Julia's choice for completing the real number line, where there are two infinities (`-1//0` and `1//0`).

I suggest to allow complex infinity for rational numbers. It doesn't really matter for my application how complex infinity is handled – it could be a single point, or it could be handled the same as for complex floating-point numbers – but I want to avoid thrown an exception.

Why?

The problem I'm encountering is with Julia's linear algebra. To test certain algorithms I don't want to have to deal with floating-point errors, and I thus use `Rational{BigInt}` and `Complex{Rational{BigInt}}` as element types. This works well in general, but the corner cases are all haywire.

For example, let's consider inverting each element of a sparse vector: `map(inv, SparseVector(...))`. The sparse vector `map` function check whether the result of `inv` applied to the element type is zero. If the element type is `Complex{Rational{BigInt}}`, then `inv` throws an exception, even if none of the elements are zero (i.e. if the sparse vector happens to be dense):

```Julia
using LinearAlgebra
using SparseArrays
julia> inv.(sparsevec([1], [1//1]))
1-element SparseVector{Rational{Int64}, Int64} with 1 stored entry:
  [1]  =  1//1
julia> inv.(sparsevec([1], [Complex(1//1)]))
ERROR: DivideError: integer division error
```
Note that this exception is spurious: The result is well-defined, Julia should return `sparsevec([1], [Complex(1//1)])` again.

Handling this (and many similar cases in other types/functions) is quite tedious. One would need to add new methods for `Base.inv`, as in
```Julia
function Base.inv(D::Diagonal{T,SparseVector{T,I}}) where {T,I}
    if length(D.diag.nzval) == D.diag.n
        # Omit check `iszero(inv(zero(T)))`
        return Diagonal(SparseVector(D.diag.n, D.diag.nzind, map(inv, D.diag.nzval)))
    else
        # Fall back to original function
        return Diagonal(map(inv, D.diag))
    end
end
```

I was considering submitting respective pull requests to Julia (for quite a few linear algebra algorithms) – `inv`, `ldiv!`, `rdiv!`, `map`,  etc., for `Diagonal`, `SparseVector`, `SparseArrayCSC`, and more. Essentially, everywhere the value `zero(T)` is used in a way that could involve an inverse or a division, that respective check needs to be delayed.

Allowing some kind of complex rational infinity instead would simplify this a lot.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46315/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46321","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46321/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46321/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46321/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46321","id":1336356098,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Ppy0C","number":46321,"title":"Wrong matrix multiplication result with Bidiagonal matrices","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-11T18:48:36Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T18:48:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is incorrect:
```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> A = Bidiagonal{Rational{BigInt}}([81//100, -9//100, 31//50], [71//100, 23//50],:U)
julia> B = Bidiagonal{Rational{BigInt}}([-7//100, 53//100, -1//10], [-9//10, -7//20], :L)
julia> C = Bidiagonal{Rational{BigInt}}([17//20, -23//25, -9//50], [-39//50,  0//1], :L)
julia> (A*B)*C - A*(B*C)
3×3 SparseMatrixCSC{Rational{BigInt}, Int64} with 2 stored entries:
  ⋅        ⋅            ⋅
  ⋅    2691//5000  88619//250000
  ⋅        ⋅            ⋅
```
The result should be zero, but it is not.

I am using
```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.6.7
Commit 3b76b25b64 (2022-07-19 15:11 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin21.4.0)
  CPU: Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-9980HK CPU @ 2.40GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-11.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
```

The problem only exists on Julia 1.6, it is correct in Julia 1.7 onwards.

```Julia
julia> using SparseArrays
julia> x = sparsevec([1], [1//1+0im])
julia> inv.(x)
ERROR: DivideError: integer division error
```

Complex rational numbers in Julia can't represent complex infinity. The `map` function for sparse vectors
pre-calculates `inv(0)` for efficiency, and that fails, even though this value is not actually needed here.

```Julia
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> using SparseArrays
julia> A = SymTridiagonal(sparsevec(Int[1]), sparsevec(Int[]))
julia> A + A
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching SymTridiagonal(::SparseVector{Int64, Int64}, ::Vector{Int64})
```

I assume the problem is that `SymTridiagonal` matrices accidentally convert the internal representation of the off-diagonal elements to a dense vector.

I am using
```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.9.0-DEV.1130
Commit c80316e125* (2022-08-15 13:05 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin21.6.0)
  CPU: 16 × Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-9980HK CPU @ 2.40GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-14.0.5 (ORCJIT, skylake)
  Threads: 8 on 16 virtual cores
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46456","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46456/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46456/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46456/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46456","id":1348082220,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5QWhos","number":46456,"title":"`kron` unnecessarily converts to dense matrices","user":{"login":"eschnett","id":213514,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIxMzUxNA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/213514?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett","html_url":"https://github.com/eschnett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eschnett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-08-23T14:48:38Z","updated_at":"2022-08-23T14:48:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`kron` returns dense matrices from some inputs types where this is not necessary:
```
julia> using LinearAlgebra
julia> A = kron(Diagonal([1,1,1]), Tridiagonal([2,2], [3,3,3], [4,4]))
9×9 Matrix{Int64}:
 3  4  0  0  0  0  0  0  0
 2  3  4  0  0  0  0  0  0
 0  2  3  0  0  0  0  0  0
 0  0  0  3  4  0  0  0  0
 0  0  0  2  3  4  0  0  0
 0  0  0  0  2  3  0  0  0
 0  0  0  0  0  0  3  4  0
 0  0  0  0  0  0  2  3  4
 0  0  0  0  0  0  0  2  3

julia> A == Tridiagonal(A)
true
```

That is, `kron` could return a `Tridiagonal` matrix here, but doesn't. The same is true for `Bidiagonal` and `SymTridiagonal` matrices. The condition here is that `kron`'s first argument is a `Diagonal` matrix.

Similarly (but less important), `kron` could return upper or lower triangular matrices in some cases:
```Julia
julia> A = kron(Bidiagonal([3,3,3], [4,4], :U), Diagonal([1,1,1]))
9×9 Matrix{Int64}:
 3  0  0  4  0  0  0  0  0
 0  3  0  0  4  0  0  0  0
 0  0  3  0  0  4  0  0  0
 0  0  0  3  0  0  4  0  0
 0  0  0  0  3  0  0  4  0
 0  0  0  0  0  3  0  0  4
 0  0  0  0  0  0  3  0  0
 0  0  0  0  0  0  0  3  0
 0  0  0  0  0  0  0  0  3

julia> A == UpperTriangular(A)
true
```

The condition here is that all input types are respective `Diagonal`, `Bidiagonal`, or `Triangular` matrices.

+@testset "kron (issue #46456)" begin
+    A = Diagonal(randn(10))
+    BL = Bidiagonal(randn(10), randn(9), :L)
+    BU = Bidiagonal(randn(10), randn(9), :U)
+    C = SymTridiagonal(randn(10), randn(9))
+    D = Tridiagonal(randn(9), randn(10), randn(9))
+    ABL = kron(A, BL)
+    ABU = kron(A, BU)
+    AC = kron(A, C)
+    AD = kron(A, D)
+    @test ABL isa Bidiagonal
+    @test ABU isa Bidiagonal
+    @test AC isa SymTridiagonal
+    @test AD isa Tridiagonal
+    @test ABL == kron(Array(A), Array(BL))
+    @test ABU == kron(Array(A), Array(BU))
+    @test AC == kron(Array(A), Array(C))
+    @test AD == kron(Array(A),
